# Derivation 3.5A — Local observer bookkeeping {#sec-derivation-03-05A-local-observer-bookkeeping .unnumbered}

## Purpose

This appendix supports Chapter 3.5. It records the local bookkeeping rules that connect the stationary field variables to the quantities read by clocks, transported particles, and local observers.

The main point is separation of roles. The stationary field is established by the source-to-field map. Local observer bookkeeping says how a particle or clock reads that field at its own location.

## Role / regime / status

- **Role:** local readout rule for the stationary M1 gravity package.
- **Regime:** stationary field, local observer patch, scalar+shift-led bookkeeping.
- **Status:** working reference convention for the gravity chapter.
- **Non-claim:** this is not a full dynamical equation of motion and not a complete nonstationary observer theory.

## Local scalar dressing

The scalar field enters local clocking through

$$
G(x)=\sqrt{1-2\theta_0(x)}.
$$

The local context-dressed fermic scale is

$$
p_{f,c}(x)=p_fG(x).
$$

This is the gravity-side clocking rule. The field does not first change an external time parameter. It changes the local cycle-carrying scale through which the clock is read.

In the weak-field limit,

$$
G(x)=1-\theta_0(x)+O(\theta_0^2),
$$

so, where $\theta_0>0$ marks a gravitational well,

$$
p_{f,c}<p_f.
$$

The local fermic scale is reduced relative to the asymptotic value. In M1 language, this is the local form of gravitational clock slowing.

## Shift-dressed transport momentum

The local transport momentum is not the bare spatial momentum alone. In the stationary scalar+shift package, define

$$
\Pi_i=p_i-p_{f,c}A_i.
$$

Here $A_i$ is the directional/shift field at the observer location. The factor $p_{f,c}$ is included because the shift correction is read through the same local clock-carrying scale that the scalar well has already dressed.

This ordering matters:

$$
\theta_0 \longrightarrow p_{f,c},
\qquad
(p_{f,c},A_i,p_i)\longrightarrow \Pi_i.
$$

The scalar dressing is not an optional correction added after transport. It sets the local scale against which shift-modified transport is read.

## Local packaged magnitude

With the local fermic scale and transport momentum in hand, the natural packaged local magnitude is

$$
M_{g,loc}(x,p)=\sqrt{p_{f,c}(x)^2+\gamma^{ij}(x)\Pi_i\Pi_j}.
$$

This expression is a local readout package. It says how the particle's fermic scale and transported momentum are read inside the stationary gravitational structure.

It should not be confused with a global conserved quantity. The local value depends on the observer location, the local field, and the chosen local readout frame.

## Stationary generator distinction

A stationary transport generator, denoted schematically by

$$
H_{g,stat},
$$

plays a different role. It organizes stationary evolution or transport in a background that has a time-independent field structure. It is therefore a generator-side object, not merely the same thing as the local magnitude $M_{g,loc}$.

The distinction can be summarized as

$$
M_{g,loc}:\ \text{local contextualized magnitude},
$$

$$
H_{g,stat}:\ \text{stationary transport/generator bookkeeping}.
$$

The two objects can be related in a stationary setting, but identifying them too early erases the difference between local readout and global/stationary organization.

## Local and asymptotic readings

Let $x_\infty$ denote an asymptotic reference region where

$$
G(x_\infty)\to1,
\qquad
A_i(x_\infty)\to0,
\qquad
\gamma^{ij}(x_\infty)\to\delta^{ij}.
$$

Then the local package tends toward the flat expression

$$
M_{g,loc}(x_\infty,p)\to\sqrt{p_f^2+\delta^{ij}p_ip_j}.
$$

Away from that reference region, the local readout is instead

$$
M_{g,loc}(x,p)=\sqrt{p_f^2G(x)^2+\gamma^{ij}(x)(p_i-p_fG(x)A_i)(p_j-p_fG(x)A_j)}.
$$

This is why the gravity chapter keeps local and asymptotic language separate. The local observer reads the field actually present at its location. The asymptotic observer supplies a comparison standard.

## Clocking and motion separation

For a clock at rest relative to the local stationary frame, the scalar dressing carries the leading gravity-side clocking effect:

$$
p_f\longrightarrow p_{f,c}=p_fG.
$$

For a moving particle, motion-side effects are read after this local dressing and relative to the local stationary frame. Thus the schematic order is

$$
\text{field}\longrightarrow\text{local clock scale}\longrightarrow\text{local transport}\longrightarrow\text{observed motion/clock comparison}.
$$

This prevents a common confusion: gravitational clock slowing and motion-side slowing should not be collapsed into one undifferentiated factor. In M1, the scalar field first changes the local cycle-carrying scale; motion is then read using the locally relevant transport bookkeeping.

## Result

The stationary observer layer is governed by three compact rules:

$$
p_{f,c}=p_fG,
$$

$$
\Pi_i=p_i-p_{f,c}A_i,
$$

$$
M_{g,loc}=\sqrt{p_{f,c}^2+\gamma^{ij}\Pi_i\Pi_j}.
$$

Together they define the local readout grammar used in Chapter 3.5. The scalar well dresses the local clock-carrying scale, the shift modifies local transport, and the packaged magnitude summarizes the local gravitationally dressed state without replacing the stationary generator.
